burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis per FA; died Kansas City MO Nov 1924

narrative_blocks.header: Born in 1830; outlived all her children
narrative_blocks.body: Henrietta Theresa Parsons Strong was born in Michigan in 1830 and died in Kansas City, Missouri in November 1924 at age ninety-three — surviving her husband George Parish Strong by thirty-seven years, the Civil War, two children who predeceased her, and the first World War, her life spanning virtually the entire arc of American expansion from the frontier Michigan of Andrew Jackson's presidency to the Model T era. She was buried at Crown Hill Cemetery in Indianapolis — the same cemetery that holds the tomb of President Benjamin Harrison — a woman who had watched three American generations unfold from a Michigan farm to a Missouri city, buried in a cemetery more famous for a president she had outlived.

narrative_blocks.body: Henrietta Theresa Parsons Strong was born in Michigan in 1830 and died in Kansas City, Missouri in November 1924 at age ninety-three — surviving her husband George Parish Strong by thirty-seven years, the Civil War, two children who predeceased her, and the first World War, her life spanning virtually the entire arc of American expansion from the frontier Michigan of Andrew Jackson's presidency to the Model T era. She was buried at Crown Hill Cemetery in Indianapolis — the same cemetery that holds the tomb of President Benjamin Harrison — a woman who had watched three American generations unfold from a Michigan farm to a Missouri city, buried in a cemetery more famous for a president she had outlived.

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery West Goshen Township Chester County per FA

career.organization: Connected with Lukens Steel, Coatesville, Pennsylvania
career.notes: Active member of Presbyterian Church; board of deacons. Per FA memorial.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the family's Hooker ancestry — his grandmother Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong brought the Hooker name into the Strong family when she married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker; his daughter married her Hooker cousin
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker Strong was named for his grandmother Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, who had brought the Hooker name into the Strong family through her 1813 marriage to Elisha Beebe Strong — and he carried that name west from Kansas, where he was born in 1871, to Pennsylvania, where he worked as an accountant connected to Lukens Steel in Coatesville and served on the board of deacons of the Presbyterian Church. He died in Altoona, Pennsylvania in 1938, outlived by a daughter and a grandson who carried the Hooker name into the Vietnam War.

narrative_blocks.header: An accountant connected with Lukens Steel in Pennsylvania
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker Strong settled in Chester County, Pennsylvania, where he worked as an expert accountant connected with Lukens Steel — the Coatesville ironworks that had been rolling armor plate since the Civil War and would become one of the nation's most storied steel producers, known for the plates that built American warships and later for pioneering stainless steel production. He was an active member of the Presbyterian Church and served on the board of deacons, maintaining the denominational tradition of his grandfather George Parish Strong and the broader Hooker family's Congregationalist and Presbyterian roots.

narrative_blocks.header: His son Henry also bore the name Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker Strong named his son Henry Hooker Strong (born 1901), carrying the name into yet another generation of the Strong family, four generations removed from Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong who first brought it into the Strongs through her 1813 marriage to Elisha Beebe Strong. Henry Hooker Strong's own son, Captain Henry Hooker Strong Jr., also bore the name — making it five consecutive generations of Hooker-named Strongs, ending with a Navy Skyhawk pilot shot down over North Vietnam in 1972.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery West Goshen Township Chester County per FA
Green Mount Cemetery West Goshen Township Chester County per FA

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the family's lineage through Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong — his great-great-grandmother — who married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813 and introduced the Hooker name into the Strong family.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker in the fourth generation of Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker Strong was the fourth consecutive generation of the Strong family to carry 'Hooker' as a given name — following his father George Hooker Strong (born 1871), his grandfather George Parish Strong (born 1824), and ultimately tracing back to Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong who first planted the name in the Strong family through her 1813 marriage. His own son, Captain Henry Hooker Strong Jr., would carry it into a fifth generation and into the Vietnam War, where he was shot down over North Vietnam in 1972 at age thirty-nine.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

death.cause: Heart failure on Monon train outside Indianapolis

narrative_blocks.header: Died of heart failure on the Monon train
narrative_blocks.body: John Winder Strong, a newspaper man, died on January 3, 1904 when his heart failed on the Monon Railroad train outside Indianapolis at age fifty-two. He left behind a wife and two sons who would serve in two World Wars: Major General George Veazey Strong, who became the Army's intelligence chief in World War II, and Captain William Robert Strong, who fought at Chateau-Thierry and was killed in a hunting accident in 1919.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons fought in two separate World Wars
narrative_blocks.body: John Winder Strong had two sons who served in different American wars: his elder son George Veazey Strong became the Army's intelligence chief in World War II as a major general, and his younger son William Robert Strong fought at Chateau-Thierry in World War I as a captain before being killed in a Montana hunting accident in 1919, leaving a daughter born two months after his death. The newspaper man who died on the Monon train in 1904 left a military legacy that spanned two world wars.

burial.plot_notes: Fort Leavenworth National Cemetery per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her journalist husband by twenty-seven years
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Veazey Strong was widowed at forty-eight when her newspaper husband John Winder Strong died on the Monon Railroad train in 1904, and spent the remaining twenty-seven years of her life as the mother of two sons who would fight in two different World Wars — the elder becoming the Army's intelligence chief, the younger dying in a hunting accident in Montana in 1919. She was buried at Fort Leavenworth National Cemetery in Kansas — a military burial ground reserved for veterans and their dependents — following her son Major General George Veazey Strong's military star long after she had outlived the newspaper man who had been her husband.

bio.title: Major General
bio.photo_notes: Major General George Veazey Strong (1880-1946), WWII Army G-2 intelligence chief

burial.plot_notes: United States Military Academy Post Cemetery West Point per FA

notable.notable_blurb: Army G-2 intelligence chief during World War II

education.school_name: Command and General Staff School, Fort Leavenworth

career.organization: United States Military Academy at West Point

career.notes: Chief of Military Mission to Preparatory Disarmament Conference 1925-1928; adviser through 1935.
HQ Omaha Nebraska. Promoted Brigadier General June 1, 1938.
Camp Bowie Texas. Promoted Major General May 1, 1941.
Oversaw Military Intelligence Service, counterintelligence, coordination with Bletchley Park. Directed Manhattan Project protection. Succeeded by Clayton Bissell February 7, 1944.

military_service.notes: Troop Movement Officer. St. Mihiel (500,000 troops) and Meuse-Argonne (1.2 million troops). First DSM 1922. WWI Victory Medal 3 clasps. Knight of French Legion of Honor.

Oversaw Military Intelligence Service. Ultra coordination with Bletchley Park. Manhattan Project counterintelligence. Operation Fortitude for D-Day. Second DSM 1944. Legion of Merit. Retired February 29, 1944.

cross_connections.display_label: Third cousins: WWII general and Eisenhower's Treasury Secretary
cross_connections.notes: HD4700 GV Strong (gen 8 through H00795 Dolly Hooker Strong) and HD4679 George Magoffin Humphrey (gen 8 through H00799 Mary Chaffee Hooker) are third cousins — both great-great-grandchildren of H00348 Captain James Hooker. Both were prominent in national affairs during the same WWII/postwar decade.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Civil War captain and WWII general
cross_connections.notes: H02027 Captain James Henry Hooker (Civil War; willed $200,000 for military school with Roosevelt as executor) and HD4700 GV Strong (WWII G-2 intelligence chief) represent the military tradition of the Hooker-Strong family across two American wars. H02027 is GV Strong's first cousin twice removed.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Army G-2 chief and software quality pioneer
cross_connections.notes: HD4700 GV Strong is the first cousin of I00957 Katherine Strong, who is the mother of HD4695 Watts Humphrey — making GV Strong and Watts Humphrey first cousins once removed. Both achieved national prominence: one as the Army's intelligence chief, one as the father of software quality.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Gilded Age poet and Army intelligence chief
cross_connections.notes: H03408 J. Hooker Hamersley (gen 7 through H00797 Judge James Hooker) and HD4700 GV Strong (gen 8 through H00795 Dolly Hooker Strong) are second cousins once removed — both descendants of H00348 Captain James Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: Third cousins: speedboat champion and WWII intelligence chief
cross_connections.notes: H04621 (gen 8 through H00797 Judge James Hooker) and HD4700 GV Strong (gen 8 through H00795 Dolly Hooker Strong) are third cousins — both great-great-grandchildren of H00348.

cross_connections.display_label: the officer who ran the 1953 Iran coup
served the upstart OSS that his G-2 fought for turf

narrative_blocks.header: Coordinated 1.2 million soldiers for the Meuse-Argonne
narrative_blocks.body: As Troop Movement Officer for the 4th Army Corps in 1918, Lieutenant Colonel George Veazey Strong coordinated the logistics of moving more than 1.2 million American soldiers across a 20-mile front for the Meuse-Argonne Offensive — and managed the entire St. Mihiel assembly (over 500,000 troops) just weeks earlier — earning his first Distinguished Service Medal for what the citation called 'tireless energy, keen foresight, and sound judgment.' He was one of the most consequential American staff officers of World War I, coordinating the largest American military operations in history to that point.

narrative_blocks.header: Army G-2 intelligence chief during World War II
narrative_blocks.body: As Assistant Chief of Staff for Intelligence (G-2) from May 1942 to February 1944, Major General Strong oversaw the Military Intelligence Service, coordinated Ultra decrypts with Bletchley Park, assigned counterintelligence units to protect the Manhattan Project at Oak Ridge and Los Alamos, and directed Operation Fortitude — the deception campaign that contributed to the success of the Normandy invasion. He received a second Distinguished Service Medal and the Legion of Merit, and his directorate produced thousands of reports on German intelligence networks that shaped Allied strategy across North Africa, Italy, and France.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent to Tokyo to study Japanese; compiled dictionaries
narrative_blocks.body: In 1908, George Veazey Strong was selected as one of the first two U.S. Army officers sent to Japan specifically to study the Japanese language, spending three years in Tokyo compiling Japanese-English dictionaries and building cultural intelligence expertise that would prove invaluable three decades later when he became the Army's intelligence chief — making his appointment as G-2 in 1942 a product not just of a long staff career but of a linguistic and cultural preparation few American officers had. He married the daughter of a Danish law professor at the Imperial Japanese College while stationed there, and his son George Loenholm Strong was born in Tokyo in 1910.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten years advising arms limits at Geneva
narrative_blocks.body: From 1925 to 1935, George Veazey Strong served as the U.S. military adviser to the American delegation at the Geneva disarmament conferences — including as chief of the military mission to the Preparatory Disarmament Conference — working through the interwar decade to define international limits on armaments while European militarism was rebuilding. His decade of arms-limitation diplomacy made him one of the most internationally experienced soldiers in the Army when he was appointed G-2 intelligence chief in 1942.

narrative_blocks.header: Shared West Point 1904 with Stilwell and McNair
narrative_blocks.body: The West Point Class of 1904 — Cullum Number 4242 for George Veazey Strong — also produced Joseph Stilwell, who commanded Allied forces in the China-Burma-India theater in World War II, and Lesley J. McNair, who became commanding general of Army Ground Forces before being killed by friendly fire in Normandy in 1944; Strong himself became the Army's intelligence chief. Three men from the same graduating class shaped three different dimensions of the American war effort, none of whom could have predicted in 1904 that their careers would converge in the same global conflict.

narrative_blocks.header: He sent agents to guard the bomb's secret
narrative_blocks.body: As G-2 chief, Major General Strong assigned dedicated counterintelligence units to the Manhattan Project's key facilities at Oak Ridge, Tennessee and Los Alamos, New Mexico — one of the earliest and most consequential applications of military intelligence tradecraft to a scientific program, aimed at preventing leaks that could have compromised atomic operations in the Pacific theater. His directorate also signed the 1942 Delimitation Agreement with the FBI and Office of Naval Intelligence that organized counterintelligence jurisdictions across agencies, and coordinated with British intelligence at Bletchley Park on the dissemination of Ultra decrypts from Enigma to field commanders without compromising the source.

narrative_blocks.header: Advised the conference that created the United Nations
narrative_blocks.body: After retiring in February 1944, George Veazey Strong was retained by the War Department as a military adviser to the Dumbarton Oaks Conference — held August through October 1944 in Washington, D.C. — where representatives from the United States, United Kingdom, Soviet Union, and China drafted the proposals that became the United Nations Charter; the former intelligence chief brought his wartime knowledge of Allied dynamics to the table at the moment the postwar world order was being designed. He subsequently advised the Inter-American Conference on Problems of War and Peace in Mexico City in 1945, his final international assignment after a military career that had taken him from Moro insurgents in the Philippines to the founding documents of the postwar world.

military_service.notes: Secretary Helena Rifle Club before war. Carried Colt automatic pistol at Chateau-Thierry. Killed in hunting accident Oct 19, 1919 near Ovando MT, witnessed by gunwriter Elmer Keith.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed hunting; daughter born two months later
narrative_blocks.body: Captain William Robert Strong served at the Battle of Chateau-Thierry in World War I carrying the Colt automatic pistol he had bought as Secretary of the Helena Rifle Club, and was shot and killed in a hunting accident north of Ovando, Montana on October 19, 1919 — gunwriter Elmer Keith, standing six feet away, recalled the fatal slug missed him by inches. His daughter Billie Louise was born on December 16, 1919, two months after his death, and died in 1925 at age five.

narrative_blocks.header: At Chateau-Thierry he did not miss a shot
narrative_blocks.body: Captain William Robert Strong fought at the Battle of Chateau-Thierry in 1918 carrying the .30-06 Colt automatic pistol he had purchased for $14.25 through the DCM and NRA as Secretary of the Helena Rifle Club before the war — and told gunwriter Elmer Keith that he 'used several clips in one engagement and did not think he missed a shot,' a soldier's account that Keith found credible enough to preserve in his book Sixguns. Keith was standing six feet away from Strong on the morning the hunting accident took his life in 1919 — 'the 150 grain .30-06 slug that took his life missed me by inches' — making him the last witness to a man who had carried the same gun through a World War.

sources.label: Elmer Keith, Sixguns — eyewitness account of the hunting accident

burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis per FA — same cemetery as his mother Henrietta Theresa Parsons Strong (I00958)

narrative_blocks.header: Buried at Crown Hill alongside his mother
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong died in Indianapolis on January 31, 1921, and was buried at Crown Hill Cemetery in Indianapolis — the same cemetery where his mother Henrietta Theresa Parsons Strong (who outlived him by three years, dying in 1924 at age ninety-three) was also interred, two members of the same family converging on the same Indiana cemetery after lives that had begun in Wisconsin and New York. He had been born on January 4, 1854 in Fond du Lac, Wisconsin — when his father George Parish Strong was still moving the family through the Midwest — and his wife Mary Elizabeth Johnson Strong died in Lafayette, Indiana in August 1915 at age forty-three, predeceasing him by nearly six years.

burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis per FA
